summary: Gruber reflects on Basic Apple Guy's February 2022 proposal to redesign macOS System Preferences as an iOS-style Settings app, which turned out to be exactly what Apple did in macOS Ventura. Gruber had initially planned to link to the proposal with skepticism, believing it would be a bad idea in practice despite looking good in theory. He never published that critique, and Apple's WWDC 2022 announcement proved Basic Apple Guy's prediction correct. After using the Ventura beta, Gruber still has reservations about the new design but notes that hearing Craig Federighi's perspective on The Talk Show made him more open-minded.
key_points: Basic Apple Guy accurately predicted Apple would rename System Preferences to Settings and redesign it with an iOS-style scrolling layout
Gruber's instinct was that the iOS-style layout would be a bad idea in practice for Mac, despite looking good in mockups
The macOS Ventura beta confirmed Gruber's initial skepticism about the redesign's practicality
Craig Federighi's explanation on The Talk Show shifted Gruber toward being more open-minded about the change
money_quotes: This sounds like a fine idea in theory, and his mockups do look great, but I think in practice it'd be a bad idea.
Basic Apple Guy's suggestion/prediction turned out to be spot-on.
In MacOS 13 Ventura, System Preferences has been replaced by Settings, and it's not merely a name change, it's a complete redesign with an iOS-style layout.
From what I've seen in Ventura developer beta 1, I wish I'd just written that short 'sounds like a fine idea in theory but a bad idea in practice' post back in February.
Hearing Craig Federighi's take has me more open-minded.
key_insight: Apple's decision to replace macOS System Preferences with an iOS-style Settings app validated an outside designer's prediction but left Gruber cautiously skeptical about whether theory translates well to practice on the Mac.
